title: Dream machine -- the next creative economy
abstract: arXiv:2606.26114v1 Announce Type: cross  Abstract: We examine the structural transformation of creative industries under generative artificial intelligence, drawing on 374 primary sources spanning policy documents, industry data, creator surveys, and platform analytics. Beginning with the December 2024 release of OpenAI's Sora video model as a watershed event, we trace the historical pattern of creative resistance to technological disruption, then develop an analytical framework -- the Human-AI Agency Continuum for mapping the spectrum of human and machine collaboration in creative work. We present evidence for the "slop ceiling," an audience-imposed quality threshold that constrains AI-generated content to approximately 1--3% of platform streams despite comprising 44% of uploads. Analysis of the UK Government's 2025 consultation on AI and copyright (over 11,500 responses, 88% opposing expanded AI training rights) reveals deep structural tensions between technology firms and creative w

Title: Dream machine -- the next creative economy
Abstract: arXiv:2606.26114v1 Announce Type: cross  Abstract: We examine the structural transformation of creative industries under generative artificial intelligence, drawing on 374 primary sources spanning policy documents, industry data, creator surveys, and platform analytics. translated_title: 梦想机器——下一个创意经济
translated_abstract: 我们基于374份原始资料（涵盖政策文件、行业数据、创作者调查和平台分析），审视了生成式人工智能下创意产业的结构性转型。以2024年12月OpenAI的Sora视频模型的发布为分水岭事件，我们追溯了创意领域对技术颠覆的历史抵抗模式，然后开发了一个分析框架——“人机能动性连续体”，用于映射创意工作中人类与机器协作的光谱。我们提出了“垃圾内容天花板”的证据，这是一个由受众设定的质量门槛，尽管AI生成内容占上传量的44%，但在平台流媒体中仅占约1%-3%。对英国政府2025年关于AI与版权的咨询（超过11,500份回复，88%反对扩大AI训练权利）的分析揭示了科技公司与创意领域之间深层次的结构性紧张关系。
tldr: 本文通过“人机能动性连续体”框架和“垃圾内容天花板”现象，揭示了生成式AI在创意经济中虽占上传量近半，却因质量门槛仅获极低流量，并基于大量政策与调查数据，凸显了科技与创意行业间的结构性矛盾。
en_tdlr: This paper introduces the "Human-AI Agency Continuum" framework and the "slop ceiling" phenomenon, revealing that while generative AI accounts for nearly half of uploads in the creative economy, it receives minimal streaming due to quality thresholds, and highlights deep structural tensions between tech and creative industries based on extensive policy and survey data.
